---
title: "Immigration"
description: "Who is arriving in Canada, from where, and through which door — permanent residents, students, temporary workers, and asylum claimants by source country and category"
---

Immigration is one of the forces most shaping Canada today. This page looks at **who arrives and from where** — the source countries and admission streams behind the headline numbers — using Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada's monthly administrative data. It is descriptive: the counts, categories, and origins, without a verdict.

It complements the [Population & Growth](index.qmd) page (the pace of population change and the non-permanent-resident share) and the [Citizenship](citizenship.qmd) page (how many become citizens). Figures are rounded to the nearest five, exclude the current partial year, and follow IRCC's small-count suppression.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
from pipeline.config import get_data_date, CATEGORICAL_COLORS
from pipeline.charts import stacked_area, category_bar, single_bar
STEEL = CATEGORICAL_COLORS[0]
```

## New permanent residents, by category

Canada admits permanent residents through several streams. The **economic** class — skilled workers and the families they bring, selected largely for labour-market reasons — is by far the largest, consistently around three-fifths of admissions. **Family** sponsorship (spouses, partners, children, and parents) is the next-largest, ahead of **refugees and protected persons**. Total admissions rose to a peak of about 470,000 in 2024, then stepped down in 2025 as the federal immigration-levels plan lowered the targets.

```{python}
#| echo: false
prc_path = "data/population/ircc_pr_by_category.csv"
if os.path.exists(prc_path):
    prc = pd.read_csv(prc_path)
    fig = stacked_area(prc, "year", "count", "category",
        yaxis_title="Permanent residents admitted",
        group_order=["Economic", "Family (sponsored)", "Refugee & protected", "Other"],
        source_note=f"Source: IRCC, Permanent Residents – Monthly Updates (open data) | Data as of: {get_data_date(prc_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("PR-by-category data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Where new permanent residents come from

The single largest source of new permanent residents, by a wide margin, is **India**, followed by the Philippines and China — a mix that reflects both the economic-class selection system and long-standing family and community ties. The chart shows the top source countries in the most recent complete year.

```{python}
#| echo: false
prk_path = "data/population/ircc_pr_by_country.csv"
if os.path.exists(prk_path):
    prk = pd.read_csv(prk_path)
    yr = int(prk["year"].max())
    fig = category_bar(prk[prk["year"] == yr], "country", "count",
        xaxis_title="Permanent residents admitted", value_fmt=",.0f", color=STEEL, top_n=15,
        source_note=f"Source: IRCC, Permanent Residents – Monthly Updates (open data) — {yr}.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("PR-by-country data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## International students, by origin

Study-permit holders are counted separately — they are temporary residents, not permanent immigrants. **India** and **China** dominate the international-student intake, ahead of a long tail of other countries. (These are new permits taking effect each year, so the totals move with the federal study-permit caps introduced in 2024.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
sk_path = "data/population/ircc_study_by_country.csv"
if os.path.exists(sk_path):
    sk = pd.read_csv(sk_path)
    yr = int(sk["year"].max())
    fig = category_bar(sk[sk["year"] == yr], "country", "count",
        xaxis_title="Study-permit holders", value_fmt=",.0f", color=STEEL, top_n=12,
        source_note=f"Source: IRCC, Temporary Residents: Study Permit Holders – Monthly Updates (open data) — {yr}.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Study-permit data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Temporary workers, by origin

Work-permit holders come through two main routes — the International Mobility Program (open work permits, including post-graduation permits and the Canada–Ukraine measures) and the Temporary Foreign Worker Program (employer-specific permits). Combined, **India** is again the largest origin, followed by Ukraine, Mexico, and the Philippines.

```{python}
#| echo: false
wk_path = "data/population/ircc_work_by_country.csv"
if os.path.exists(wk_path):
    wk = pd.read_csv(wk_path)
    yr = int(wk["year"].max())
    fig = category_bar(wk[wk["year"] == yr], "country", "count",
        xaxis_title="Work-permit holders (IMP + TFWP)", value_fmt=",.0f", color=STEEL, top_n=12,
        source_note=f"Source: IRCC, Temporary Residents: Work Permit Holders – Monthly Updates (open data) — {yr}.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Work-permit data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Asylum claims

A person who reaches Canada and claims protection is an **asylum claimant** — a separate process from the resettlement of refugees selected abroad (counted in the permanent-resident totals above). Asylum claims rose steeply from 2022, peaking near **172,000 in 2024**, before easing in 2025. This flow is the source of the large pending caseload often cited in the news; that backlog — the *stock* of claims awaiting a decision at the Immigration and Refugee Board — is a different measure from the annual *flow* shown here, and is not published as a clean, regularly-updating series.

```{python}
#| echo: false
at_path = "data/population/ircc_asylum_total.csv"
if os.path.exists(at_path):
    at = pd.read_csv(at_path)
    fig = single_bar(at, "year", "count", title="", yaxis_title="Asylum claims",
        color=STEEL, hover_fmt=",.0f",
        source_note=f"Source: IRCC, Asylum Claimants – Monthly Updates (open data) | Data as of: {get_data_date(at_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Asylum-total data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

### Where asylum claimants come from

The leading countries of citizenship for recent claimants are **Haiti** and **India**, followed by Iran, Nigeria, and Mexico. (IRCC reports the top 25 countries per province, so the very small origins are grouped out.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
ak_path = "data/population/ircc_asylum_by_country.csv"
if os.path.exists(ak_path):
    ak = pd.read_csv(ak_path)
    yr = int(ak["year"].max())
    fig = category_bar(ak[ak["year"] == yr], "country", "count",
        xaxis_title="Asylum claimants", value_fmt=",.0f", color=STEEL, top_n=12,
        source_note=f"Source: IRCC, Asylum Claimants – Monthly Updates (open data) — {yr}.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Asylum-by-country data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

### How claims are decided

Of the claims the Immigration and Refugee Board finalizes each year, the share **accepted** — granted refugee protection — has risen markedly, from about **44% in 2013 to roughly 80%** in recent years. (The acceptance rate counts positive decisions as a share of positive plus negative; claims abandoned or withdrawn are excluded.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
ao_path = "data/population/ircc_asylum_outcomes.csv"
if os.path.exists(ao_path):
    ao = pd.read_csv(ao_path)
    fig = single_bar(ao, "year", "acceptance_rate", title="",
        yaxis_title="Acceptance rate", color=STEEL, hover_fmt=".1f", yticksuffix="%",
        source_note=f"Source: Immigration and Refugee Board, Refugee Protection Division decisions (open data) | Data as of: {get_data_date(ao_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Asylum-outcomes data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Download data

- [Permanent residents by category (CSV)](../data/population/ircc_pr_by_category.csv) — IRCC Permanent Residents – Monthly Updates
- [Permanent residents by source country (CSV)](../data/population/ircc_pr_by_country.csv) — IRCC Permanent Residents – Monthly Updates
- [Study-permit holders by source country (CSV)](../data/population/ircc_study_by_country.csv) — IRCC Temporary Residents – Monthly Updates
- [Work-permit holders by source country (CSV)](../data/population/ircc_work_by_country.csv) — IRCC Temporary Residents – Monthly Updates
- [Asylum claims, total by year (CSV)](../data/population/ircc_asylum_total.csv) — IRCC Asylum Claimants – Monthly Updates
- [Asylum claimants by source country (CSV)](../data/population/ircc_asylum_by_country.csv) — IRCC Asylum Claimants – Monthly Updates
- [Asylum decision outcomes by year (CSV)](../data/population/ircc_asylum_outcomes.csv) — Immigration and Refugee Board, Refugee Protection Division

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## About this data
All figures are from Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada's monthly open-data releases (Open Government Licence – Canada). Counts are rounded to the nearest five and small values are suppressed, so totals may not add exactly. "Permanent residents" are people admitted for permanent residence; study- and work-permit holders and asylum claimants are temporary residents counted on a different basis. Source-country detail uses country of citizenship.
:::
